title: "Security Architecture"

::: {.callout-note}
## Scope Note: Regulatory Compliance

This architecture document describes security and privacy features inspired by regulatory requirements (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA), but **does not claim full legal compliance**. The implementation follows privacy-by-design principles and security best practices, but full regulatory compliance requires legal counsel, formal Data Protection Impact Assessments, organizational governance, and compliance certification beyond the scope of this academic project.

**What IS implemented**: Security best practices, privacy-by-design, user data control, audit logging, basic GDPR rights (export, delete)  
**What IS NOT implemented**: Legal compliance certification, formal breach notification (72-hour), DPIAs, regulatory audit trails for compliance reporting

References to "GDPR Article" or "compliance" throughout this document should be understood as architectural inspiration, not legal certification.
:::


## Security Layers (Defense in Depth)

```{mermaid}
graph TB
    Request[Incoming Request] --> L1
    
    subgraph L1["Layer 1: Network Security"]
        Firewall[Firewall Rules]
        DDoS[DDoS Protection]
        IP[IP Allowlisting]
    end
    
    subgraph L2["Layer 2: Transport Security"]
        TLS[TLS 1.3]
        Cert[Certificate Validation]
        HSTS[HSTS Headers]
    end
    
    subgraph L3["Layer 3: Authentication"]
        JWT[Custom JWT Validation]
        Social[Social OAuth - Hybrid]
        MFA[Multi-Factor Auth - TOTP]
        Session[Session Management - Redis]
    end
    
    subgraph L4["Layer 4: Authorization"]
        Scopes[OAuth 2.1 Scopes]
        RBAC[Role-Based Access Control]
        ABAC[Attribute-Based Access Control]
        RLS[Row-Level Security]
    end
    
    subgraph L5["Layer 5: API Security"]
        RateLimit[Rate Limiting]
        APIKey[API Key Validation]
        Signing[Request Signing]
        CORS[CORS Policies]
    end
    
    subgraph L6["Layer 6: Application Security"]
        InputVal[Input Validation]
        OutputEnc[Output Encoding]
        SQLPrev[SQL Injection Prevention]
        XSSPrev[XSS Prevention]
    end
    
    subgraph L7["Layer 7: Monitoring"]
        IDS[Intrusion Detection]
        Anomaly[Anomaly Detection]
        Audit[Security Audit Reviews]
    end
    
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> Response[Secure Response]
    
    style L1 fill:#ffebee
    style L2 fill:#fce4ec
    style L3 fill:#f3e5f5
    style L4 fill:#ede7f6
    style L5 fill:#e8eaf6
    style L6 fill:#e3f2fd
    style L7 fill:#e0f2f1
    style Request fill:#fff3e0
    style Response fill:#e8f5e9
```


## Authentication Architecture

### Custom JWT Token Strategy

The system uses **custom JWT-based authentication** with full control over token lifecycle.

**Implementation Status:**

- **auth_users Table**: COMPLETED (Dec 29, 2025) - Database schema with 1:1 relationship to base_profiles
- **Email Verification**: Infrastructure ready (Mailpit at http://127.0.0.1:54324 for local testing)
- **Password Hashing**: Schema ready, Argon2id implementation pending in MAS-26 Auth Service
- **JWT Tokens**: Pending implementation in MAS-27 Auth Endpoints

```
User Authentication Flow (Password-Based):
   |
   |-> POST /api/v1/auth/login
   |   |-> Validate credentials
   |   |-> Verify password with Argon2id
   |   |-> Check account status
   |
   |-> Custom Auth Service Issues JWT
   |   |-> Access token (15 minutes expiry)
   |   |-> Refresh token (7 days expiry)
   |   |-> Store session in Redis
   |
   |-> Access Token Contains:
   |   |-> sub (user_id from auth_users.id)
   |   |-> email
   |   |-> account_type (verified/unverified/pseudonymous)
   |   |-> roles [] (for RBAC)
   |   |-> iat (issued at)
   |   |-> exp (expiration)
   |   |-> jti (unique token ID)
   |
   +-> API validates token on each request
       |-> Verify signature (HS256 or RS256)
       |-> Check expiration
       |-> Validate against session store (Redis)
       |-> Set PostgreSQL RLS context
       +-> Extract user context for authorization
```

**auth_users Table Schema (IMPLEMENTED Dec 29, 2025):**

```sql
CREATE TABLE auth_users (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id UUID NOT NULL UNIQUE REFERENCES base_profiles(user_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE WHERE deleted_at IS NULL,
    password_hash TEXT NOT NULL,  -- Argon2id (m=65536, t=3, p=4)
    
    -- Email verification
    is_email_verified BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
    email_verified_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    verification_token TEXT,
    verification_token_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    
    -- Login protection
    last_login_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    failed_login_attempts INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    locked_until TIMESTAMPTZ,  -- Account lockout for brute-force prevention
    password_changed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    
    -- Password reset
    reset_token TEXT,
    reset_token_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    
    -- Audit fields
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ  -- Soft delete with 30-day grace period
);
```

**Indexes Implemented:**

- Primary key on `id`
- Unique constraint on `user_id` (1:1 with base_profiles)
- Unique constraint on `email` (active accounts only)
- Indexes on: `email`, `user_id`, `verification_token`, `reset_token`, `locked_until`, `deleted_at`

**Seed Data:**

- 5 sample users (Sarah Chen, Li Ming, Alex, Sukarno, Jordan Smith)
- Li Ming: Unverified with stub token for testing email verification flow
- Others: Verified accounts ready for authentication testing

### Social Authentication Flow (Hybrid Approach)

```
Social Login Flow (Google/GitHub/Apple):
   |
   |-> User clicks "Login with Google"
   |-> Redirect to /api/v1/auth/social/google
   |
   |-> Supabase OAuth Configuration Handles:
   |   |-> OAuth redirect to Google
   |   |-> User authorizes
   |   |-> Google returns to callback
   |   +-> Supabase processes OAuth response
   |
   |-> Custom Callback Endpoint /api/v1/auth/callback/google
   |   |-> Receive provider user data
   |   |-> Extract email, name, provider_id
   |
   |-> Check auth_social_connections table
   |   |-> Connection exists? -> Link to existing user
   |   |-> New user? -> Create in auth_users
   |   +-> Email conflict? -> Require account linking
   |
   |-> Issue Custom JWT Tokens
   |   |-> Same format as password auth
   |   |-> Store session in Redis
   |   +-> Return to client
   |
   +-> User authenticated with custom tokens
       (not Supabase Auth tokens)
```

**Why Hybrid Approach:**

- **Leverage Supabase**: OAuth provider configuration and redirect handling
- **Maintain Control**: User data in custom tables, custom JWT format
- **No Lock-in**: Can migrate OAuth handling later if needed
- **Unified Experience**: Same token format for all auth methods
- **RLS Compatible**: Works seamlessly with PostgreSQL RLS policies

### OAuth 2.1 Authorization Code Flow (with PKCE)

```
Authorization Code Flow with PKCE (mandatory in OAuth 2.1):
   |
   |-> Client generates code_verifier (random string)
   |-> Client generates code_challenge = SHA256(code_verifier)
   |
   |-> Authorization Request:
   |   |-> GET /oauth/authorize
   |   |-> client_id, redirect_uri, scope, state
   |   |-> code_challenge, code_challenge_method=S256
   |   +-> response_type=code
   |
   |-> User authorizes third-party app
   |-> System issues authorization code (10 min expiry)
   |
   |-> Token Exchange:
   |   |-> POST /oauth/token
   |   |-> code, client_id, redirect_uri
   |   |-> code_verifier (to verify PKCE challenge)
   |   +-> grant_type=authorization_code
   |
   +-> Access Token Response:
       |-> access_token (1 hour expiry)
       |-> refresh_token (with rotation)
       |-> token_type: "Bearer"
       |-> scope: granted scopes
       |-> expires_in: 3600
       +-> context_profile_id (if context-specific)

OAuth 2.1 Improvements Over 2.0:
   |-> PKCE mandatory for all clients (prevents auth code interception)
   |-> Implicit flow removed (insecure)
   |-> Password grant removed (insecure)
   |-> Refresh token rotation enforced
   |-> Stricter redirect URI matching
   +-> Bearer token usage clarified
```


## Authorization Architecture

### Multi-Level Authorization

```
Request with Token
   |
   |-> Layer 1: Authentication Check
   |   +-> Is token valid and not expired?
   |
   |-> Layer 2: Role-Based Check (RBAC)
   |   +-> Does user have required role?
   |
   |-> Layer 3: Attribute-Based Check (ABAC)
   |   +-> Context-specific rules
   |
   |-> Layer 4: Scope-Based Check (OAuth)
   |   +-> Does token have required scope?
   |
   +-> Layer 5: Row-Level Security (RLS)
       +-> Database-enforced policies
```

### Authorization Patterns

| Pattern | Use Case | Example |
|---------|----------|---------|
| **Resource Owner** | User accesses own data | `user_id == current_user.id` |
| **Role-Based** | Admin access | `'admin' IN current_user.roles` |
| **Relationship-Based** | Guardian access | `guardian_of(current_user, target_user)` |
| **Scope-Based** | OAuth client access | `required_scope IN token.scopes` |
| **Context-Based** | Profile visibility | `profile.is_public OR user_authorized` |


## Data Protection

### Encryption Strategy

```
Data in Transit:
   +-> TLS 1.3
       |-> Strong cipher suites
       |-> Certificate validation
       +-> HSTS enforced

Data at Rest:
   |-> Database encryption (TDE)
   |-> Field-level encryption (sensitive)
   |   |-> Legal names (AES-256-GCM)
   |   +-> Date of birth
   +-> Backup encryption

Password Hashing:
   +-> bcrypt or Argon2id
       |-> Work factor: 12+ rounds
       +-> Salt per password
```

### Sensitive Data Classification

| Data Type | Classification | Protection |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Legal Name | Highly Sensitive | Encrypted + access logged |
| Date of Birth | Sensitive | Encrypted + age only exposed |
| Email | Sensitive | TLS only + masked in logs |
| Preferred Name | Public | User-controlled visibility |
| Pronouns | Public | No special protection |
| Deprecated Names | Highly Sensitive | Suppressed + encrypted + never in public APIs |


## Deadname Protection

### Overview and Rationale

Deadnaming (using a person's former name, especially for transgender individuals) causes documented psychological harm and correlates with increased suicide risk. Research by the Trevor Project (2021) demonstrates that proper name usage directly correlates with reduced suicide attempt rates among transgender youth.

The system implements comprehensive deadname protection mechanisms to prevent inadvertent or malicious exposure of deprecated names.

### Protection Mechanisms

#### 1. Name Deprecation Flag

All name records include an `is_deprecated` boolean flag:

```sql
-- IdentityNames table
CREATE TABLE identity_names (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  identity_id UUID REFERENCES base_profiles(user_id),
  name_type VARCHAR(50),
  name_value JSONB,
  is_primary BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  is_deprecated BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,  -- Marks names as sensitive/deprecated
  visibility_level VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'public',  -- public, private, historical_suppressed
  valid_from TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  valid_to TIMESTAMPTZ,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

#### 2. Visibility Levels

Three visibility levels control name exposure:

| Visibility Level | Meaning | API Exposure | Audit Log Display |
|------------------|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| `public` | Current active name | Yes (based on scopes) | Yes |
| `private` | User-restricted name | Owner only | Yes (owner only) |
| `historical_suppressed` | Deprecated/deadname | Never via API | Suppressed |

#### 3. Automatic Suppression Rules

**User-Facing Audit Logs:**

- Names marked `is_deprecated: true` are automatically redacted
- Historical entries show: `[Previous name - suppressed for privacy]`
- User can see "a name was changed" but not the old value

**Admin Audit Logs:**

- Full history retained for legal compliance
- Access requires elevated permissions
- All access to deprecated names is logged

**Public APIs:**

- Deprecated names NEVER returned via OAuth endpoints
- Profile history endpoints filter deprecated names
- Temporal queries (point-in-time) respect deprecation

#### 4. Name Change Workflow with Protection

**User-initiated name change:**
```python
# API Request
PATCH /api/v1/users/usr_123/profile
{
  "names": {
    "given": {"en": "Alex"},
    "family": {"en": "Johnson"}
  },
  "deprecate_previous_names": true  # Mark old names as deprecated
}

# System actions:
# 1. Mark previous name record: is_deprecated = true, visibility_level = 'historical_suppressed'
# 2. Create new name record: is_deprecated = false, visibility_level = 'public'
# 3. Create audit log entry (old name encrypted in admin-only logs)
# 4. Clear all caches containing old name
```

**Audit log entry (user-visible):**
```json
{
  "event_type": "profile.name_changed",
  "timestamp": "2025-10-20T10:30:00Z",
  "user_id": "usr_123",
  "changes": {
    "name": {
      "from": "[Previous name - suppressed for privacy]",
      "to": "Alex Johnson"
    }
  }
}
```

**Audit log entry (admin-only):**
```json
{
  "event_type": "profile.name_changed",
  "timestamp": "2025-10-20T10:30:00Z",
  "user_id": "usr_123",
  "changes": {
    "name": {
      "from": "Alice Johnson",  // Encrypted at rest
      "to": "Alex Johnson"
    }
  },
  "sensitivity": "deadname_change",
  "access_restricted": true
}
```

#### 5. Guardian Name Change Permissions

Guardian relationships include specific permission for name changes on minor accounts:

```json
{
  "guardian_permissions": {
    "can_view_profile": true,
    "can_edit_profile": true,
    "can_change_name": false,  // Separate permission for name changes
    "can_change_legal_name": false,  // Even more restricted
    "can_view_name_history": false  // Cannot see deprecated names
  }
}
```

**Rationale**: Name changes for minors require additional scrutiny to prevent abuse. Minor must consent to name changes (if above consent age), or require two guardians to approve.

#### 6. Legal Name Field Protection

The `legal_name` field has additional protections:

**Storage:**

- Encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM
- Separate encryption key from general database encryption
- Key rotation every 90 days

**Access:**

- NEVER exposed via OAuth (even with full scopes)
- Only accessible directly to user via authenticated endpoint
- Accessible in legal/healthcare contexts with explicit consent
- All access logged with legal basis (GDPR Article reference)

**Example - Legal name NOT included in OAuth response:**
```python
# Third-party requests profile with full scope
GET /api/v1/oauth/profile
Authorization: Bearer <token_with_profile:read:full>

# Response (legal_name excluded)
{
  "user_id": "usr_123",
  "preferred_name": "Alex Johnson",
  "display_name": "Alex Johnson",
  "email": "alex@example.com",
  "bio": "Software engineer",
  "photo_url": "https://storage/photo.jpg"
  // legal_name: NEVER INCLUDED via OAuth
}

# User can access their own legal_name directly
GET /api/v1/users/usr_123/profile?include_legal=true
Authorization: Bearer <user_own_token>

# Response includes legal_name (user accessing own data)
{
  "legal_name": "Alexander James Johnson",  // Decrypted for owner only
  ...other fields
}
```

#### 7. GDPR Right to Erasure for Deprecated Names

Users can invoke GDPR Article 17 (Right to Erasure) specifically for deprecated names:

```python
# Request permanent deletion of deprecated name
DELETE /api/v1/users/usr_123/names/deprecated
Authorization: Bearer <user_token>

# System actions:
# 1. Pseudonymize deprecated name in audit logs (hash + salt)
# 2. Physically delete name_value from identity_names table
# 3. Retain metadata (timestamp, that a name existed) for audit integrity
# 4. Create compliance log entry
```

**Result:**

- Historical audit trail shows "Name changed on [date]" but not the actual name
- Legal compliance maintained (proof that name was changed)
- No way to recover deprecated name (irreversible)

#### 8. Implementation Checklist

**Database Layer:**

- [x] Add `is_deprecated` boolean to `identity_names` table
- [x] Add `visibility_level` enum to `identity_names` table
- [x] Create index on `is_deprecated` for query performance
- [x] Implement triggers to prevent accidental is_deprecated=false updates

**Application Layer:**

- [x] Filter deprecated names in all profile resolution logic
- [x] Redact deprecated names in user-visible audit logs
- [x] Implement separate guardian permission for name changes
- [x] Add legal_name encryption/decryption in repository layer

**API Layer:**

- [x] Never expose deprecated names via OAuth endpoints
- [x] Provide user option to deprecate names on update
- [x] Implement GDPR erasure endpoint for deprecated names
- [x] Add deprecation flag to name change endpoints

**Testing:**

- [x] Test deprecated name filtering across all contexts
- [x] Test audit log redaction for deprecated names
- [x] Test legal_name never appears in OAuth responses
- [x] Test GDPR erasure for deprecated names

### Policy Summary

| Scenario | Behavior |
|----------|----------|
| User changes name | Previous name automatically marked `is_deprecated: true` |
| User views own audit log | Deprecated names shown as "[suppressed]" |
| Admin views audit log | Deprecated names visible (with access logging) |
| OAuth app requests profile | Deprecated names NEVER included |
| Guardian views minor profile | Cannot see deprecated names |
| Point-in-time historical query | Deprecated names excluded |
| GDPR erasure request | Deprecated names physically deleted |
| Legal/compliance query | Metadata retained, name pseudonymized |

### Research Citations

This design is informed by:

- **Trevor Project (2021)**: Correlation between proper name usage and reduced suicide attempts
- **Haimson (2016)**: Harm caused by real-name policies and deadnaming on social platforms
- **van der Nagel (2015)**: Importance of identity control for marginalized populations


## Security Controls

### Input Validation
- Schema validation (Pydantic)
- Length limits enforced
- Character set restrictions
- Parameterized queries (SQL injection prevention)
- Output encoding (XSS prevention)

### Rate Limiting

| Endpoint Type | Rate Limit | Window |
|--------------|------------|--------|
| Authentication | 5 requests | Per minute |
| OAuth token | 10 requests | Per minute |
| Profile read | 100 requests | Per minute |
| Profile write | 30 requests | Per minute |
| Admin operations | 60 requests | Per minute |

### Security Headers
```
X-Frame-Options: DENY
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains
Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'
X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block
```


## OWASP Top 10 Mitigations

| Vulnerability | Mitigation |
|--------------|------------|
| **Broken Access Control** | RLS + authorization checks in every endpoint |
| **Cryptographic Failures** | TLS 1.3 + AES-256 + proper key management |
| **Injection** | Parameterized queries + input validation |
| **Insecure Design** | Security by design + threat modeling |
| **Security Misconfiguration** | Secure defaults + automated scanning |
| **Vulnerable Components** | Dependency scanning + regular updates |
| **Authentication Failures** | MFA + strong password policy + rate limiting |
| **Data Integrity Failures** | Code signing + integrity checks |
| **Logging Failures** | Comprehensive audit logs + monitoring |
| **SSRF** | Input validation + network segmentation |


## Audit Logging

### What Gets Logged

```
Every operation logs:
   |-> Who (user_id, actor_id)
   |-> What (operation, resource)
   |-> When (timestamp)
   |-> Where (IP address, user agent)
   |-> Why (legal basis, GDPR article)
   +-> Changes (before/after state)
```

### Audit Log Requirements
- **Immutable**: Write-once storage
- **Encrypted**: At rest
- **Access-controlled**: Audit team only
- **Retained**: 7 years minimum
- **Tamper-evident**: Hash chaining
